sentences.chinese: 賢材雖未久，不害為輔佐。
sentences.literal: Though worthy talent has not been long in office, it does not harm being assistant and support.
sentences.idiomatic: Even a brief tenure need not bar a capable man from high counsel.

sentences.chinese: 是以有司竭力盡知，務治其業而以赴功。
sentences.literal: Therefore the authorities exhaust strength and exhaust knowledge, striving to order their tasks and to rush to achievement.
sentences.idiomatic: So officials strain every nerve on their duties and race to show results.

sentences.chinese: 今則不然。
sentences.literal: Now it is not so.
sentences.idiomatic: Today the opposite holds.

sentences.chinese: 累日以取貴，積久以致官，是以廉恥貿亂，賢不肖渾淆，未得其真。
sentences.literal: They pile up days to obtain honor, accumulate length of time to reach office—therefore integrity and shame are traded in confusion, worthy and unworthy are muddled together, and the true is not obtained.
sentences.idiomatic: Rank comes from seniority, office from long sitting—so honor and shame are confused, worthies blur with fools, and the court never sees men plain.

sentences.chinese: 臣愚以為使諸列侯、郡守、二千石各擇其吏民之賢者，歲貢各二人以給宿衛，且以觀大臣之能；
sentences.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks: cause the various marquises, commandery governors, and two-thousand-bushel officials each to select the worthy among their clerks and people, yearly each recommending two persons to supply palace guard duty, and thereby observe the ability of great ministers;
sentences.idiomatic: I would have every noble, governor, and two-thousand-bushel official nominate two local talents each year for palace service, and so test how well the great ministers seek men.

sentences.chinese: 所貢賢者有賞，所貢不肖者有罰。
sentences.literal: for those who recommend the worthy there is reward; for those who recommend the unworthy there is punishment.
sentences.idiomatic: Reward honest nominations, punish empty names.

sentences.chinese: 夫如是，諸侯、吏二千石皆盡心於求賢，天下之士可得而官使也。
sentences.literal: If it is like this, then the marquises and two-thousand-bushel clerks all exhaust their minds in seeking the worthy, and the gentlemen of all under Heaven can be obtained and employed in office.
sentences.idiomatic: Then every lord and governor will hunt talent in earnest, and the empire’s scholars can be placed in office as they deserve.

sentences.chinese: 遍得天下之賢人，則三王之盛易為，而堯、舜之名可及也。
sentences.literal: If one universally obtains the worthy men of all under Heaven, then the splendor of the Three Kings is easy to practice, and the names of Yao and Shun can be reached.
sentences.idiomatic: Gather the realm’s worthies and the glory of the Three Kings is within reach, the fame of Yao and Shun no longer a distant echo.

sentences.chinese: 毋以日月為功，實試賢能為上，量材而授官，錄德而定位，則廉恥殊路，賢不肖異處矣。
sentences.literal: Do not take sun and moon as merit; truly testing worth and ability is supreme, measuring material and conferring office, recording virtue and fixing position—then integrity and shame walk different roads, and worthy and unworthy dwell in different places.
sentences.idiomatic: Forget mere seniority; test real ability, match office to gifts and rank to character—then honor and shame part company, and the worthy stand clear of the mean.

sentences.chinese: 陛下加惠，寬臣之罪，令勿牽制於文，使得切磋究之，臣敢不盡愚！
sentences.literal: Your Majesty adds favor, widens your servant’s crime, orders not to be tied up by documents, so as to allow polishing and investigating it—your servant dares not exhaust his foolishness!
sentences.idiomatic: You have shown grace, forgiven my bluntness, and told me not to stay bound by formal prose so I can argue the matter through—I shall not hold back.

sentences.chinese: 於是天子復冊之。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven again enfeoffed him with a document.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor then sent a third rescript.

sentences.chinese: 制曰：蓋聞「善言天者必有征於人，善言古者必有驗於今」。
sentences.literal: The edict said: I have heard “He who speaks well of Heaven must have evidence in man; he who speaks well of antiquity must have verification in the present.”
sentences.idiomatic: The rescript read: “They say, ‘He who explains Heaven must prove it on human beings; he who explains the past must prove it in the present.’”

sentences.chinese: 故朕垂問乎天人之應，上嘉唐虞，下悼桀、紂，浸微浸滅浸明浸昌之道，虛心以改。
sentences.literal: Therefore I hang my questions on the response of Heaven and man, above commending Tang and Yu, below grieving Jie and Zhou Xin, the way of gradually fading and gradually perishing, gradually brightening and gradually flourishing—with empty mind to reform.
sentences.idiomatic: So I ask how Heaven answers man, praising Yao and Shun above, mourning Jie and Zhou below, tracing how states fade or flourish—hoping to mend my ways with an open heart.

sentences.chinese: 今子大夫明於陰陽所以造化，習於先聖之道業，然而文采未極，豈惑乎當世之務哉？
sentences.literal: Now you, great officers, are clear on how yin and yang thereby transform and nourish, practiced in the way and undertakings of the former sages—yet literary pattern is not yet utmost; are you perhaps puzzled by the tasks of the present age?
sentences.idiomatic: You know yin and yang’s work and the sages’ teaching, yet your answers still feel thin—are you holding back on what today most needs?

sentences.chinese: 條貫靡竟，統紀未終，意朕之不明與？
sentences.literal: The strip threading through is not finished, the binding thread is not ended—is it that my intent is not clear?
sentences.idiomatic: The thread of your argument still hangs loose—is my question unclear?

sentences.chinese: 聽若眩與？
sentences.literal: Or is what I hear like dizziness?
sentences.idiomatic: Or do my ears spin when I listen?

sentences.chinese: 夫三王之教所祖不同，而皆有失，或謂久而不易者道也，意豈異哉？
sentences.literal: As for the teachings of the Three Kings, what they took as ancestor differed, yet each had loss; some say what long endures without change is the Way—is the intent perhaps different?
sentences.idiomatic: The Three Kings each built on a different past, each had blind spots; some say the unchanging core is the Way—do you read that differently?

sentences.chinese: 今子大夫既已著大道之極，陳治亂之端矣，其悉之究之，孰之復之。
sentences.literal: Now you, great officers, have already written the utmost of the Great Way and stated the beginnings of order and chaos—exhaust them, investigate them, settle them, repeat them.
sentences.idiomatic: You have traced the Great Way and the roots of order and chaos—now push further: nail each point down, then say it again plain.

sentences.chinese: 《詩》不雲乎，「嗟爾君子，毋常安息，神之聽之，介爾景福。」
sentences.literal: Does not the Odes say, “Ah, you gentlemen, do not constantly seek ease; the spirits listen to it, and will enlarge your bright blessing.”
sentences.idiomatic: The Odes warn, “Gentlemen, do not lounge in comfort—the spirits are listening, and great blessing follows the wakeful.”

sentences.chinese: 朕將親覽焉，子大夫其茂明之。
sentences.literal: I will personally review it; you, great officers, should luxuriantly make it clear.
sentences.idiomatic: I shall read every word; give your answers in full, rich detail.”

sentences.chinese: 仲舒復對曰：
sentences.literal: Zhongshu again replied, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu answered again:

sentences.chinese: 臣聞《論語》曰：「有始有卒者，其唯聖人虖！」
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard the Analects says: “From beginning to end in one piece—perhaps only the sage!”
sentences.idiomatic: The Analects says, “To see a thing through from start to finish—that belongs to the sage alone.”

sentences.chinese: 今陛下幸加惠，留聽於承學之臣，復下明冊，以切其意，而究盡聖德，非愚臣之所能具也。
sentences.literal: Now Your Majesty has fortunately added favor, stayed his ear on a minister who receives learning, again sent down a clear document to cut to his meaning, and exhausted utmost sage virtue—it is not what a foolish minister can fully set forth.
sentences.idiomatic: You have stooped to hear a student of the classics and sent a second, sharper rescript—demands no humble scholar can fully meet.

sentences.chinese: 前所上對，條貫靡竟，統紀不終，辭不別白，指不分明，此臣淺陋之罪也。
sentences.literal: In the reply submitted before, the strip threading through was not finished, the binding thread was not ended, the wording was not distinguished clear, the points were not distinct and bright—this is your servant’s shallow and mean crime.
sentences.idiomatic: My first answer trailed off, tangled its thread, blurred its terms—that was my fault for shallow learning.

sentences.chinese: 冊曰：「善言天者必有征於人，善言古者必有驗於今。」
sentences.literal: The document said: “He who speaks well of Heaven must have evidence in man; he who speaks well of antiquity must have verification in the present.”
sentences.idiomatic: Your text said, “Explain Heaven on the evidence of man; prove antiquity by what we see today.”

sentences.chinese: 臣聞天者群物之祖也。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that Heaven is the ancestor of the throng of things.
sentences.idiomatic: Heaven is father to the myriad things.

sentences.chinese: 故遍覆包涵而無所殊，建日月風雨以和之，經陰陽寒暑以成之。
sentences.literal: Therefore it everywhere covers, wraps, and contains them without distinguishing, establishes sun, moon, wind, and rain to harmonize them, threads yin and yang, cold and heat to complete them.
sentences.idiomatic: It roofs all creation without partiality, sets sun and moon and wind and rain to tune them, and weaves cold and heat through yin and yang to ripen them.

sentences.chinese: 故聖人法天而立道，亦溥愛而亡私，布德施仁以厚之，設誼立禮以導之。
sentences.literal: Therefore the sage models Heaven and establishes the Way; he also broadly loves without selfishness, spreads virtue and extends benevolence to enrich them, sets up righteousness and establishes ritual to guide them.
sentences.idiomatic: The sage takes Heaven as his model: boundless care without private favor, kindness to feed the people, duty and rites to lead them.

sentences.chinese: 春者天之所以生也，仁者君之所以愛也；
sentences.literal: Spring is that whereby Heaven gives birth; benevolence is that whereby the lord cherishes.
sentences.idiomatic: Spring is Heaven’s birth-gift; humaneness is how the ruler loves.

sentences.chinese: 夏者天之所以長也，德者君之所以養也；
sentences.literal: Summer is that whereby Heaven grows; virtue is that whereby the lord nurtures.
sentences.idiomatic: Summer is Heaven’s growth; nurturing character is the ruler’s part.

sentences.chinese: 霜者天之所以殺也，刑者君之所以罰也。
sentences.literal: Frost is that whereby Heaven kills; punishment is that whereby the lord fines.
sentences.idiomatic: Frost is Heaven’s killing edge; penalties are how the ruler checks wrong.

sentences.chinese: 繇此言之，天人之征，古今之道也。
sentences.literal: From this speaking, the sign of Heaven and man is the way of antiquity and present.
sentences.idiomatic: Thus the echo between Heaven and man is the same bridge from past to present.

sentences.chinese: 孔子作《春秋》，上揆之天道，下質諸人情，參之於古，考之於今。
sentences.literal: Confucius made the Chunqiu, above matching it to Heaven’s Way, below interrogating it in human feeling, joining it to antiquity, examining it in the present.
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn Annals with one eye on Heaven’s pattern, one on human passion, checked against old ages and today.

sentences.chinese: 故《春秋》之所譏，災害之所加也；
sentences.literal: Therefore what the Chunqiu ridicules is where disaster and harm attach;
sentences.idiomatic: What the Annals mocks, Heaven strikes;

sentences.chinese: 《春秋》之所惡，怪異之所施也。
sentences.literal: what the Chunqiu hates is where the strange and anomalous apply.
sentences.idiomatic: what the Annals condemns, portents visit.

sentences.chinese: 書邦家之過，兼災異之變；
sentences.literal: It records the faults of states and houses, together with the changes of disasters and prodigies;
sentences.idiomatic: It inscribes the failings of houses and kingdoms beside their calamities and omens;

sentences.chinese: 以此見人之所為，其美惡之極，乃與天地流通而往來相應，此亦言天之一端也。
sentences.literal: by this is seen that what men do, at the utmost of their beauty and ugliness, flows and interchanges with Heaven and earth and responds back and forth—this also is one end of speaking of Heaven.
sentences.idiomatic: so we see human conduct, pushed to its best or worst, trades breath with Heaven and earth—another way of “speaking Heaven.”

sentences.chinese: 古者修教訓之官，務以德善化民，民已大化之後，天下常亡一人之獄矣。
sentences.literal: In antiquity there were officials who cultivated instruction and training, striving with virtue and goodness to transform the people; after the people were greatly transformed, all under Heaven constantly had not one man in prison.
sentences.idiomatic: Antiquity set officers to teach, moved the people by goodness until, fully transformed, the empire often held not a single prisoner.

sentences.chinese: 今世廢而不修，亡以化民，民以故棄行誼而死財利，是以犯法而罪多，一歲之獄以萬千數。
sentences.literal: In the present age it is cast aside and not cultivated; there is nothing wherewith to transform the people; the people therefore cast aside conduct and righteousness and die for wealth and profit—therefore they violate the law and crimes are many; in one year’s prisons they are in the tens of thousands.
sentences.idiomatic: We let that office rot, so the people ditch duty for profit, break the law in droves, and fill the jails by tens of thousands a year.

sentences.chinese: 以此見古之不可不用也，故《春秋》變古則譏之。
sentences.literal: By this is seen that antiquity cannot be not used; therefore the Chunqiu, when antiquity is changed, ridicules it.
sentences.idiomatic: The Annals jeer whenever a state abandons the old model—Heaven’s lesson is that the ancient machinery must be used.

sentences.chinese: 天令之謂命，命非聖人不行；
sentences.literal: What Heaven commands is called fate; fate does not move without the sage;
sentences.idiomatic: Heaven’s command is fate, and fate does not stir without a sage;

sentences.chinese: 質樸之謂性，性非教化不成；
sentences.literal: what is plain and simple is called nature; nature does not complete without transformation through instruction;
sentences.idiomatic: the raw endowment is nature, and nature is not finished without teaching;

sentences.chinese: 人欲之謂情，情非度制不節。
sentences.literal: what man desires is called feeling; feeling is not restrained without measure and regulation.
sentences.idiomatic: appetite is feeling, and feeling runs wild without measure and law.

sentences.chinese: 是故王者上謹於承天意，以順命也；
sentences.literal: Therefore the king, above, is careful in receiving Heaven’s intent, thereby to comply with fate;
sentences.idiomatic: So the true king above heeds Heaven’s mind to answer his mandate;

sentences.chinese: 下務明教化民，以成性也；
sentences.literal: below he strives to clarify transformation through instruction for the people, thereby to complete nature;
sentences.idiomatic: below he clarifies moral teaching to perfect human nature;

sentences.chinese: 正法度之宜，別上下之序，以防欲也；
sentences.literal: he corrects what is fitting in laws and measures, distinguishes the order of high and low, thereby to guard desire;
sentences.idiomatic: he sets just laws, marks rank high and low, and fences desire;

sentences.chinese: 修此三者，而大本舉矣。
sentences.literal: cultivate these three, then the great root is raised.
sentences.idiomatic: do those three and the great root is planted.

sentences.chinese: 人受命於天，固超然異於群生，入有父子兄弟之親，出有君臣上下之誼，會聚相遇，則有耆老長幼之施，粲然有文以相接，歡然有恩以相愛，此人之所以貴也。
sentences.literal: Man receives the mandate from Heaven; he is indeed loftily different from the throng of the living—within he has the closeness of father and son, elder and younger brothers; without he has the righteousness of lord and minister, high and low; when they meet and gather, there is the extending of old and young; brightly there is pattern by which to connect, gladly there is grace by which to love one another—this is why man is honored.
sentences.idiomatic: Man, charged by Heaven, stands above the creatures: at home he has the ties of kin, abroad the order of ruler and subject; in company he shows respect for age; he moves in clear ritual and warm kindness—that is his nobility.

sentences.chinese: 生五穀以食之，桑麻以衣之，六畜以養之，服牛乘馬，圈豹檻虎，是其得天之靈，貴於物也。
sentences.literal: He grows the five grains to feed them, mulberry and hemp to clothe them, the six domestic animals to nurture them; he yokes oxen and rides horses, pens leopards and cages tigers—this is his obtaining Heaven’s numinous power and being honored above things.
sentences.idiomatic: He grows grain for food, silk for clothes, herds for meat, harnesses oxen and horses, cages beasts—gifts of Heaven’s spirit that set him above the brutes.

sentences.chinese: 故孔子曰：「天地之性人為貴。」
sentences.literal: Therefore Confucius said: “Among the natures of Heaven and earth, man is honored.”
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Of all nature between Heaven and earth, none is nobler than man.”

sentences.chinese: 明於天性，知自貴於物；
sentences.literal: Clear on Heaven’s nature, he knows to honor himself above things;
sentences.idiomatic: Knowing Heaven’s intent for man, he knows his own dignity;

sentences.chinese: 知自貴於物，然後知仁誼；
sentences.literal: knowing to honor himself above things, then he knows benevolence and righteousness;
sentences.idiomatic: that dignity found, he grasps humaneness and right;

sentences.chinese: 知仁誼，然後重禮節；
sentences.literal: knowing benevolence and righteousness, then he esteems ritual and moderation;
sentences.idiomatic: with humaneness and right, he cherishes ritual and measure;

sentences.chinese: 重禮節，然後安處善；
sentences.literal: esteeming ritual and moderation, then he peacefully dwells in the good;
sentences.idiomatic: cherishing ritual, he rests in the good;

sentences.chinese: 安處善，然後樂循理；
sentences.literal: peacefully dwelling in the good, then he delights in following pattern;
sentences.idiomatic: resting in the good, he takes joy in the Way;

sentences.chinese: 樂循理，然後謂之君之。
sentences.literal: delighting in following pattern, then he is called fit to be lord (gentleman).
sentences.idiomatic: joying in the Way, he earns the name of gentleman.

sentences.chinese: 故孔子曰「不知命，亡以為君子」，此之謂也。
sentences.literal: Therefore Confucius said “Without knowing fate, there is no way to be a gentleman”—this speaks of it.
sentences.idiomatic: Hence Confucius said, “Without knowing Heaven’s charge, one cannot be a gentleman”—he meant this ladder of self-cultivation.

sentences.chinese: 冊曰：「上嘉唐、虞，下悼桀、紂，浸微浸滅浸明浸昌之道，虛心以改。」
sentences.literal: The document said: “Above commending Tang and Yu, below grieving Jie and Zhou Xin, the way of gradually fading and gradually perishing, gradually brightening and gradually flourishing—with empty mind to reform.”
sentences.idiomatic: Your rescript asked me to praise Yao and Shun, mourn Jie and Zhou, and trace how kingdoms wither or wax—so you may reform with an open heart.”

sentences.chinese: 臣聞眾少成多，積小致臣，故聖人莫不以晻致明，以微致顯。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that the many are completed from the few, and the great is reached by accumulating the small; therefore the sage none does not use dimness to reach brightness, use the slight to reach the manifest.
sentences.idiomatic: Many grains of sand make a tower; the sage turns faint beginnings into blazing success.

sentences.chinese: 是以堯發於諸侯，舜興乎深山，非一日而顯也，蓋有漸以致之矣。
sentences.literal: Therefore Yao rose from the marquises, Shun rose from deep mountains—not in one day did they become manifest; probably there was gradualness by which it was brought about.
sentences.idiomatic: Yao rose from a fief, Shun from the wildwood—neither leaped to fame in a day; both climbed by slow degrees.

sentences.chinese: 言出於已，不可塞也；
sentences.literal: Words that issue from oneself cannot be stopped;
sentences.idiomatic: A word once spoken cannot be chased home;

sentences.chinese: 行發於身，不可掩也。
sentences.literal: acts that issue from the body cannot be covered.
sentences.idiomatic: a deed once done cannot be hidden.

sentences.chinese: 言行，治之大者，君子之所以動天地也。
sentences.literal: Words and acts are the greatest in ordering; they are how the gentleman moves Heaven and earth.
sentences.idiomatic: Speech and conduct are government’s hinge—they are how a gentleman shakes Heaven and earth.

sentences.chinese: 故盡小者大，慎微者著。
sentences.literal: Therefore he who exhausts the small becomes great; he who is careful of the slight becomes illustrious.
sentences.idiomatic: Mind the small to grow great, watch the subtle to shine bright.

sentences.chinese: 《詩》云：「惟此文王，小心翼翼。」
sentences.literal: The Odes says: “Only this King Wen, careful and cautious.”
sentences.idiomatic: The Odes say of King Wen, “He walked in fear and care.”

sentences.chinese: 胡堯兢兢日行其道，而舜業業日致其孝，善積而名顯，德章而身尊，以其浸明浸昌之道也。
sentences.literal: Thus Yao daily walked his way in fear and trembling, and Shun daily extended his filial piety in earnest toil; goodness accumulated and name became manifest, virtue shone and the person was honored—by the way of gradually brightening and gradually flourishing.
sentences.idiomatic: So Yao trod the Way with trembling care, Shun piled up filial deeds day by day—goodness stacked until their names shone, virtue plain until all honored them: the path of slow, sure growth.

sentences.chinese: 積善在身，猶長日加益，而人不知也；
sentences.literal: Goodness accumulated in the person is like the long day adding increase, yet people do not know it;
sentences.idiomatic: Goodness in the body lengthens like a summer day—yet no one notices the stretch;

sentences.chinese: 積惡在身，猶火之銷膏，而人不見也。
sentences.literal: evil accumulated in the person is like fire melting fat, yet people do not see it.
sentences.idiomatic: evil eats the marrow like fire on lard—yet the victim never sees the burn.

sentences.chinese: 非明乎情性察乎流俗者，孰能知之？
sentences.literal: Unless one is clear on feeling and nature and examines the floating customs, who can know it?
sentences.idiomatic: Only a man who reads passion and probes fashion can see it coming.

sentences.chinese: 此唐、虞之所以得令名，而桀、紂之可為悼懼者也。
sentences.literal: This is how Tang and Yu obtained fine names, and how Jie and Zhou may be grieved and feared.
sentences.idiomatic: That is how Yao and Shun won praise, and how Jie and Zhou became warnings.

sentences.chinese: 夫善惡之相從，如景鄉之應形聲也。
sentences.literal: As for good and evil following one another, it is like shadow and echo responding to form and sound.
sentences.idiomatic: Good and evil trail each other as shadow and echo answer body and voice.

sentences.chinese: 故桀、紂暴謾，讒賊並進，賢知隱伏，惡日顯，國日亂，晏然自以如日在天，終陵夷而大壞。
sentences.literal: Therefore Jie and Zhou Xin were violent and slow; slanderous thieves advanced together; the worthy and wise hid and lay low; evil daily became manifest, the state daily chaotic; they were complacent as if like the sun in Heaven—finally they slid down and were greatly ruined.
sentences.idiomatic: Jie and Zhou grew cruel and slack; flatterers swarmed while sages hid; evil mounted, the realm rotted, yet they lolled as if the sun would never set—then came the crash.

sentences.chinese: 夫暴逆不仁者，非一日而亡也，亦以漸至，故桀、紂雖亡道，然猶享國十餘年，此其浸微浸滅之道也。
sentences.literal: As for the violent, contrary, and inhumane, they do not perish in one day; it also arrives by gradualness—therefore although Jie and Zhou had lost the Way, still they enjoyed the state more than ten years: this is their way of gradually fading and gradually perishing.
sentences.idiomatic: Tyrants do not fall overnight; Jie and Zhou clung to the throne years after the Way was gone—such is the slow slide to ruin.

sentences.chinese: 冊曰：「三王之教所祖不同，而皆有失，或謂久而不易者道也，意豈異哉？」
sentences.literal: The document said: “The teachings of the Three Kings, what they took as ancestor differed, yet each had loss; some say what long endures without change is the Way—is the intent perhaps different?”
sentences.idiomatic: You also asked whether the Three Kings’ different models each failed, yet some call the unchanging core “the Way”—do those views clash?

sentences.chinese: 臣聞夫樂而不亂復而不厭者謂之道；
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that what is joyful without disorder, repeated without satiety is called the Way;
sentences.idiomatic: What stays ordered yet never cloys, returns yet never wearies—that is the Way;

sentences.chinese: 道者萬世之弊，弊者道之失也。
sentences.literal: the Way is the flaw of ten thousand generations; the flaw is the Way’s loss.
sentences.idiomatic: the Way runs through all ages; “flaw” only means a moment when the Way slips.

sentences.chinese: 先王之道必有偏而不起之處，故政有眊而不行，舉其偏者以補其弊而已矣。
sentences.literal: The way of the former kings necessarily has places where it leans and does not rise; therefore government has dimness and does not go—raise its leaning part to repair its flaw, that is all.
sentences.idiomatic: Every old program tilts somewhere; when policy dims, lift the weak side and patch the crack—nothing more.

sentences.chinese: 三王之道所祖不同，非其相反，將以救溢扶衰，所遭之變然也。
sentences.literal: The ways of the Three Kings, what they took as ancestor differed, not because they opposed one another, but because they intended to rescue overflow and support decline—the changes they encountered were so.
sentences.idiomatic: The Three Kings differed because each age needed a different brace—not because their goals contradicted.

sentences.chinese: 故孔子曰：「亡為而治者，其舜乎！」
sentences.literal: Therefore Confucius said: “He who does nothing yet orders—perhaps only Shun!”
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said, “To rule without fuss—perhaps only Shun managed it.”

sentences.chinese: 改正朔，易服色，以順天命而已；
sentences.literal: Correct the first month of the year, change garment color, thereby to comply with Heaven’s mandate, that is all;
sentences.idiomatic: Shun merely fixed the calendar and court dress to answer Heaven;

sentences.chinese: 其餘盡循堯道，何更為哉！
sentences.literal: for the rest he wholly followed Yao’s way—what more change was there!
sentences.idiomatic: the rest followed Yao’s path unchanged.

sentences.chinese: 故王者有改制之名，亡變道之實。
sentences.literal: Therefore the king has the name of reforming institutions, but no reality of changing the Way.
sentences.idiomatic: So kings may “reform” rites, yet never touch the Way itself.

sentences.chinese: 然夏上忠，殷上敬，周上文者，所繼之救，當用此也。
sentences.literal: Yet Xia honored loyalty, Yin honored reverence, Zhou honored pattern—the succor they inherited required using these.
sentences.idiomatic: Xia stressed plain good faith, Shang awe, Zhou refinement—each repaired what the last age had spoiled.

sentences.chinese: 孔子曰：「殷因於夏禮，所損益可知也；
sentences.literal: Confucius said: “Yin inherited Xia ritual—the subtractions and additions can be known;
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Shang took Xia’s rites—what it dropped or added can be known;

sentences.chinese: 周因於殷禮，所損益可知也；
sentences.literal: Zhou inherited Yin ritual—the subtractions and additions can be known;
sentences.idiomatic: Zhou took Shang’s rites—again the shifts can be traced;

sentences.chinese: 其或繼周者，雖百世可知也。」
sentences.literal: he who may succeed Zhou—even after a hundred generations it can be known.”
sentences.idiomatic: and whoever follows Zhou, even a hundred reigns later, can be foretold.”

sentences.chinese: 此言百王之用，以此三者矣。
sentences.literal: This saying is that the use of the hundred kings takes these three.
sentences.idiomatic: He meant every dynasty’s tool kit comes down to those three shifts.

sentences.chinese: 夏因於虞，而獨不言所損益者，其道如一而所上同也。
sentences.literal: Xia inherited Yu; alone it does not speak of subtractions and additions because its Way was one and what it honored was the same.
sentences.idiomatic: Xia took Yu’s legacy whole, so the Annals name no tweaks—the Way stayed one.

sentences.chinese: 道之大原出於天，天不變，道亦不變，是以禹繼舜，舜繼堯，三聖相受而守一道，亡救弊之政也，故不言其所損益也。
sentences.literal: The great origin of the Way comes from Heaven; Heaven does not change, the Way also does not change—therefore Yu succeeded Shun, Shun succeeded Yao, the three sages received in succession and guarded one Way; there was no government of rescuing flaws—therefore it does not speak of their subtractions and additions.
sentences.idiomatic: The Way springs from Heaven; Heaven is changeless, so the Way is changeless—Yao, Shun, and Yu handed on one thread with no “repair” edicts, hence silence on additions or cuts.

sentences.chinese: 繇是觀之，繼治世者其道同，繼亂世者其道變。
sentences.literal: From this viewed: he who succeeds an ordered age, his Way is the same; he who succeeds a chaotic age, his Way changes.
sentences.idiomatic: Who follows peace may keep the same Way; who inherits chaos must change the recipe.

sentences.chinese: 今漢繼大亂之後，若宜少損周之文致，用夏之忠者。
sentences.literal: Now Han succeeds after great chaos; it seems fitting slightly to reduce Zhou’s literary polish and employ Xia’s loyalty.
sentences.idiomatic: Han comes after total ruin; it should trim Zhou’s ornament and recover Xia’s plain loyalty.

sentences.chinese: 陛下有明德嘉道，愍世欲之靡薄，悼王道之不昭，故舉賢良方正之士，論議考問，將欲興仁誼之林德，明帝王之法制，建太平之道也。
sentences.literal: Your Majesty has bright virtue and fine ways, pities the age’s desire becoming thin, grieves that the kingly way is not shining clear; therefore you raise worthy and upright men, discuss, debate, examine, and inquire, intending to raise the forest virtue of benevolence and righteousness, clarify the laws and institutions of emperors and kings, and establish the way of great peace.
sentences.idiomatic: Your luminous virtue grieves for a drifting age and a half-hidden royal road, so you summon the worthy and upright to debate policy—aiming to grow the grove of humaneness and duty, set out true kingship, and build the road to great peace.

sentences.chinese: 臣愚不肖，述所聞，誦所學，道師之言，廑能勿失耳。
sentences.literal: Your servant is foolish and unworthy, narrates what he has heard, recites what he has studied, conveys the words of his teachers—only able not to lose them.
sentences.idiomatic: I am unworthy: I repeat what I was taught and hope not to garble my masters.

sentences.chinese: 若乃論政事之得失，察天下之息耗，此大臣輔佐之職，三公九卿之任，非臣仲舒所能及也，然而臣竊有怪者。
sentences.literal: If it comes to discussing gain and loss in governing affairs, examining the world’s rest and exhaustion—that is the duty of great ministers assisting and supporting, the charge of the three dukes and nine ministers—not what your servant Zhongshu can reach; yet your servant privately has what is strange.
sentences.idiomatic: Weighing policy, measuring the realm’s pulse—that belongs to the high ministers, not to me—yet one thing puzzles me.

sentences.chinese: 夫古之天下亦今之天下，今之天下亦古之天下，共是天下，古以大治，上下和睦，習俗美盛，不令而行，不禁而止，吏亡奸邪，民亡盜賊，囹圄空虛，德潤草木，澤被四海，鳳皇來集，麒麟來游，以古准今，壹何不相逮之遠也！
sentences.literal: As for the world of antiquity also being the world of today, the world of today also being the world of antiquity—they share being the world; antiquity used it to achieve great order, high and low harmonious, customs beautiful and flourishing, without command yet acting, without prohibition yet stopping, clerks had no treachery and deviance, the people had no robbers and bandits, prisons were empty, virtue moistened plants and trees, bounty covered the four seas, phoenixes came to gather, unicorns came to roam—using antiquity to measure today, how is it that they do not reach so far!
sentences.idiomatic: Antiquity’s world is today’s world—yet antiquity knew great peace: custom fair, orders needless, jails empty, omens kind, phoenix and unicorn at court. Match that to now—why the chasm?

sentences.chinese: 安所繆□而陵夷若是？
sentences.literal: Wherein is the error that we slide down thus?
sentences.idiomatic: What twist in the road has brought us so low?

sentences.chinese: 意者有所失於古之道與？
sentences.literal: Perhaps there is something lost in the way of antiquity?
sentences.idiomatic: Have we dropped some thread of the old Way?

sentences.chinese: 有所詭於天之理與？
sentences.literal: Something twisted in the pattern of Heaven?
sentences.idiomatic: Or twisted Heaven’s pattern?

sentences.chinese: 試跡之於古，返之於天，黨可得見乎。
sentences.literal: Try tracing it in antiquity, turning it in Heaven—perhaps it can be seen.
sentences.idiomatic: Search the past and scan Heaven’s logic—then the cause may show.

sentences.chinese: 夫天亦有所分予，予之齒者去其角，傅其翼者兩其足，是所受大者不得取小也。
sentences.literal: As for Heaven, it also apportions gifts: to those it gives teeth it removes horns; to those it fits with wings it pairs two feet—this is that what receives the large cannot take the small.
sentences.idiomatic: Heaven parcels gifts with a sparing hand: hornless where teeth are sharp, two-legged where wings grow—great endowments do not stack small ones.
